coredata.dc:title: 0D bio-based carbon dots and 2D MXene hybridization toward fabricating flame-retardant, conductive and sensing cellulose fabrics
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Lan
Sun, Ling
Yu, Jinke
Cao, Yufei
Liu, Xiaohui
Ren, Yuanlin
Li, Yuesheng
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of the smart era, there is an urgent need to replace traditional cellulosic textiles with flame retardant multifunctional cellulosic textiles. Herein, two novel multifunctional textiles (MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L) were obtained by constructing coatings consisting of MXene and bio-based carbon dot mixtures (CDG and CDM) on cellulose fabrics using a one-pot impregnation method. MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited desirable flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing properties due to the synergistic effect of MXene and carbon dot mixtures. In the vertical burning test, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L showed self-extinguishing when the external ignition source was removed, with limiting oxygen index values increasing from 18.1 % of untreated fabric to 34.4 % and 36.0 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the conductivities of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were as high as 31.8 S m−1 and 21.4 S m−1, respectively. Notably, benefiting from the inherent flexibility of cellulose fabrics, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were able to monitor human movement in a stable and continuous manner. In addition, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L retained good integration properties including flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing property after 20 washing cycles. In conclusion, the strategy reported in this work provided a new idea for the development of environment-friendly and economical multifunctional smart textiles.

Preparation flowchart of MXene-based fabrics (a); SEM images of C/L (b, b1), MXene-C/L (b, b2), MXene@CDG-C/L (b, b3) and MXene@CDM-C/L (b, b4); FTIR spectra (c) and XRD diffraction patterns (d, e) of all fabrics.

Cone calorimetry tests for untreated and treated fabrics (a-f).
TG curves for untreated and treated fabrics (a) and corresponding DTG curves (b); TG curves for CDM, CDG and Ti3C2Tx MXene (c).

XPS spectra before and after burning of MXene@CDG-C/L; XRD diffraction patterns of char residues for MXene@CDG-C/L (b); 3D TG-IR of C/L (c, c1) and MXene@CDG-C/L (c, c2); The absorption intensity of all fabrics at specific wavenumbers: CO (d), C-H (e), C-O-C (f) and CO2 (g); A possible flame-retardant mechanism for MXene@CDG-C/L or MXene@CDM-C/L fabrics (h).

Conductive digital photographs of MXene@CDG-C/L when placed horizontally (a, a1) and under external force (a, a2); Electrical conductivity of all fabrics (b); Schematic diagram of MXene@CDG-C/L conductivity (c). The change in resistance of MXene@CDG-C/L (d, e) and MXene@CDM-C/L (f, g) during repeated finger bending and fist clenching; A possible motion-sensing mechanisms (h).

Key data for cone calorimetry tests of untreated and treated fabrics.

head.ce:title: 0D bio-based carbon dots and 2D MXene hybridization toward fabricating flame-retardant, conductive and sensing cellulose fabrics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of the smart era, there is an urgent need to replace traditional cellulosic textiles with flame retardant multifunctional cellulosic textiles. Herein, two novel multifunctional textiles (MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L) were obtained by constructing coatings consisting of MXene and bio-based carbon dot mixtures (CDG and CDM) on cellulose fabrics using a one-pot impregnation method. MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited desirable flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing properties due to the synergistic effect of MXene and carbon dot mixtures. In the vertical burning test, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L showed self-extinguishing when the external ignition source was removed, with limiting oxygen index values increasing from 18.1 % of untreated fabric to 34.4 % and 36.0 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the conductivities of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were as high as 31.8 S m−1 and 21.4 S m−1, respectively. Notably, benefiting from the inherent flexibility of cellulose fabrics, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were able to monitor human movement in a stable and continuous manner. In addition, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L retained good integration properties including flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing property after 20 washing cycles. In conclusion, the strategy reported in this work provided a new idea for the development of environment-friendly and economical multifunctional smart textiles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that fire is one of humans' greatest friends, but also one of their greatest enemies. In China, an average of several hundred thousand fire incidents occur each year, resulting in approximately 1,000 fatalities and economic losses exceeding 2 billion yuan [1]. Consequently, fire seriously endangers human life and property. Cellulose fabric, being the most abundant source of textiles, has evolved into an indispensable necessity in human production and daily life. Due to its outstanding comfort, breathability, and flexibility, cellulose is extensively utilized in various fields such as clothing, home textiles, healthcare, and military applications [2,3]. Unfortunately, cellulose is the quintessential carbohydrate with the same inherent fire risk during its utilization. To improve the fire safety of cellulose fabrics, many flame retardants have been developed [4,5], of which Pyrovatex CP flame retardant is the most commonly used [6,7]. However, cellulose fabric treated with Pyrovatex CP may potentially release free formaldehyde during use, posing significant risks to human health and contradicting the principles of green environmental protection. Traditional nitrogen-phosphorus flame retardants were being gradually developed to replace Pyrovatex CP flame retardants [8,9]. However, the fundamental source of traditional nitrogen-phosphorus flame retardants is non-renewable resources such as oil and coal. In today's serious depletion of energy resources, the design of new green flame-retardant materials and the exploration of efficient and green flame-retardant technology is an important direction for future development.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials developed after carbon-based materials such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds, and graphene, with sizes of less than 10 nm, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and tunable fluorescence [10,11]. Importantly, the CDs are abundantly available from sources that are typically bio-based materials found in nature, including grapefruit, mulberries, egg shells, straw, and more [12]. Since their discovery in 2004, CDs have been applied in a variety of fields, including catalysis, biology, electrochemistry, and sensors [13–15]. In recent years, researchers have discovered that CDs can also serve as an ideal flame-retardant material. CDs of different elemental compositions exert different flame retardant mechanisms during the combustion process, for example, P, N-doped carbon dots can catalyze the formation of char layer from the substrate as well as the release of non-combustible gases, thus inhibiting the flame spread [16]. As of 2023, CDs have been successfully employed in flame retardant studies on polyethene terephthalate [17], polyvinyl alcohol [18], polyurethane (PU) [19], polyacrylonitrile (PAN) [20], and cotton fabrics. For example, Sharatinia et al. successfully introduced the CD synthesized with citric acid as a precursor into PAN [20]. The peak heat release rate (PHRR) of PAN composites was reduced by 15.4 % when the CD was added at 2 %. This study demonstrated that CD acted as a novel, low-cost, green and efficient flame retardant and smoke suppressant for PAN. In addition, Liu et al. explored the flame-retardant application of CD in PU foams and confirmed that a denser carbon layer was formed during polymer combustion due to the extremely small particle size of CD [19]. In summary, CDs showed potential for more efficient and non-toxic flame retardancy. With the improvement of large-scale preparation technology, CDs are expected to be utilized for the development of fourth-generation emerging green flame retardants [11,13].

With the advent of the intelligent era, flame retardants for cellulose fabrics are no longer solely required to possess environmentally friendly characteristics but are also increasingly demanded to exhibit multifunctionality and durability. Typically, to ensure the safety of firefighters during the rescue process, firefighting supplies not only need to have favourable flame-retardant properties, but also need certain conductive and sensing properties. Ti3C2Tx MXene is a two-dimensional (2D) layered nanomaterial that combines various properties such as electrical conductivity and electromagnetic shielding [21,22]. Currently, Ti3C2Tx MXene is maturing for applications in conductivity and sensing [23,24]. Ti3C2Tx-treated fabrics exhibited excellent pressure-sensing properties and durability because of the hydrogen bonding between Ti3C2Tx and a variety of fabrics, as reported by Wang et al [25]. In addition, Ti3C2Tx MXene has been emerging in the flame-retardant field. Song's team has used Ti3C2Tx MXene in the flame-retardant modification of a variety of polymers, including polylactic acid, polystyrene, and polyurethane, which provided a substantial foundation for the preparation of multifunctional MXene nanocomposites [26–28]. Nevertheless, despite Ti3C2Tx MXene's satisfactory multi-functionality, it still suffers problems such as agglomeration, oxidation susceptibility, and high cost. Therefore, addressing the inherent limitations of Ti3C2Tx MXene and reducing its dosage while ensuring its functionality has been a persistent challenge. Functionalization of the Ti3C2Tx MXene surface is an effective strategy. Recently, Wang et al. developed a hybridized nanocomposite with good electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic properties by hybridizing CD with Ti3C2Tx MXene [29]. The modification of Ti3C2Tx MXene with CD not only avoided the rapid oxidation of MXene but also further enhanced the ability of MXene to detect nitrite. Similarly, CDs have been used to enhance Ti3C2Tx MXene's performance such as bio-detection and preservation [30–33]. Consequently, based on the coordination of Ti3C2Tx MXene and CDs in various fields such as flame retardancy, electrical conductivity, and sensing and corrosion protection, MXene@CDs hybrid materials are expected to be utilized for the development of greener and more economical multifunctional flame-retardant materials.

In this work, MXene@CD coatings were utilized for the multifunctional modification of cellulose fabrics. Firstly, two different carbon dot mixtures (CDG and CDM) were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method using well-sourced grapefruit peels and mulberries as precursors. Subsequently, MXene@CD coated fabrics (MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L) were fabricated at room temperature by directly adding CDG or CDM to the MXene-modified cellulose fabrics system. MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited satisfactory flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing property. On the one hand, the vertical burning tests, thermogravimetric tests and cone calorimetry tests showed that MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L had good self-extinguishing, high charring and low heat release due to the flame-retardant effect of Ti3C2Tx MXene combined with carbon dot mixtures in the condensed and gas phases. On the other hand, based on the synergistic conductivity of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were used as sensors for detecting human motion. Most importantly, Ti3C2Tx MXene acted as a bridge, and achieved not only hybridization with carbon dot but also hydrogen bonding with cellulose, and thus MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited good washing durability. This work provided a green and environmentally friendly approach to fabricate the next generation of smart fire-safe cellulose fabrics, which exhibit promising applications in smart protection, home automation, and medical health monitoring.

Ti3AlC2 powder (200 mesh, 98 %) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Lithium fluoride (LiF, 99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 %) was obtained from Tianjin Kemi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Black plain cotton/lyocell blend fabrics (C/L, 50/50, 232 g m−2) were supplied by Tiangong University. Grapefruit peels and mulberries were from the local lifestyle supermarket.

Ti3C2Tx MXene was synthesized following the method described by Wang et al [25]. Initially, LiF (1.6 g) was completely dissolved in an aqueous solution of HCl (15 mL, 9 M). Subsequently, Ti3AlC2 (1 g) was slowly added to the above solution over a period of 30 min, and the solution was continuously stirred at 35 °C for 48 h to complete the etching of Ti3AlC2. Next, the etched mixture was dispersed in deionized water followed by repeated centrifugation (3500 r min−1, 3 min) until the pH of the supernatant reached 7. The precipitate obtained after centrifugation was collected and dispersed in deionized water for sonication. Finally, the Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheet suspension was obtained after centrifugation (6000 r min−1, 30 min).

The carbon dot mixtures were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method, with the carbonization of grapefruit peels as an example. Specifically, the dried grapefruit peels (5 g) were sheared and added to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor. Subsequently, deionized water (20 mL) was introduced into the reactor, and the grapefruit peels underwent carbonization at 220 °C for 12 h. The resulting mixture was high-speed centrifugation (6000 r min−1), yielding an upper layer of brownish-yellow liquid which constituted the carbon dot mixture (CDG). Similarly, carbonization of mulberry was carried out using the above method and the final carbon dot mixture obtained was named CDM. Notably, the carbon dot mixtures were further purified to obtain pure CDs (CDg and CDm), and the specific steps were documented in Supporting Information.

Firstly, the C/L (3.5 g) were immersed in an aqueous dispersion of Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets (200 mL, 0.01 g mL−1) and were treated for 3 h at room temperature to allow complete binding of Ti3C2Tx MXene and C/L. Next, the CDG (2.0 g) was added to the above system and the reaction was continued for 12 h. Finally, the treated fabrics were rinsed with tap water followed by drying at 60 °C and designated MXene@CDG-C/L. Similarly, the fabrics treated with Ti3C2Tx MXene and CDM in combination were noted as MXene@CDM-C/L, and the fabrics treated with MXene, CDG and CDM only were named MXene-C/L, CDG-C/L, CDM-C/L, respectively. MXene@CDG-C/Lw and MXene@CDM-C/Lw were defined as MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L after 20 washing cycles. In addition, the fabrics prepared by increasing the concentration of MXene or carbon dot mixtures were named CDG-C/L (48), CDg-C/L (60), and MXene-C/L (39), MXene@CDG-C/L (30), respectively. The weight gain (WG) values of all fabrics were recorded in Table 1 and Table S1.
Please refer to the supporting information for this section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of MXene, carbon dots and carbon dots mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process and morphology of Ti3C2Tx MXene was illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Ti3C2Tx MXene was prepared by selectively etching Ti3AlC2 using HF, and exhibited a typical lamellar structure with a transverse dimension of approximately 200 nm to 500 nm in the TEM image. Furthermore, the XRD diffraction pattern of Ti3C2Tx MXene (Fig. 1c) showed an attenuated (104) diffraction peak, indicating the aluminum layer in the Ti3AlC2 was etched by HF. Meanwhile, the (002) peak of Ti3C2Tx MXene located at a lower 2 theta than that of Ti3AlC2. Based on the Bragg relation, the shift of the (002) diffraction peak indicated that Ti3C2Tx MXene had a large interlayer spacing [23], implying that Ti3AlC2 was successfully etched and stripped.

Carbon dot mixtures (CDG and CDM) and carbon dots (CDg and CDm) were obtained by hydrothermal carbonization of bio-based materials, as shown in Fig. 1b. The CDg and CDm were purified from the CDG and CDM, and the TEM images of CDg (Fig. 1b) and CDm (Fig. S1a1) revealed homogeneous and dispersed granules a particle size distribution of about 5–20 nm. The DLS results (Fig. S1b, c) validated that the average particle sizes of CDg and CDm were 6.34 nm and 10.43 nm, respectively. The XRD was utilized to further characterize the structure of the carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD diffraction patterns of both CDg and CDm had a broad diffraction peak between 20° and 33°, which corresponded to the (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon [15]. Interestingly, compared to the CDm, the XRD diffraction pattern of CDg displayed some sharp peaks at 23°, 27°, 28°, 30° and 33°, demonstrating that CDg exhibited an ordered crystal structure [18]. In addition, as depicted in Fig. S1d, the XRD diffraction patterns of CDG and CDM showed diffraction peaks caused by CDg and CDm, respectively, indicating that the carbon dot mixtures contain a large number of carbon dots [12,34]. In summary, the TEM, DLS and XRD results confirmed that CDg, CDm, CDG, and CDM were successfully prepared. The differences in chemical composition between CDg and CDm, CDG and CDM were studied using XPS. The XPS spectra of CDg and CDm displayed that CDg was composed of C, O, N and P (Fig. 1e), whereas CDm was composed of C, O, and N (Fig. 1f). The C1s, O1s, N1s and P2p high-resolution spectra of CDg were depicted in Fig. 1g. For the C1s spectrum, the peaks of CO, C-N/C-PO and C-C/CC groups appeared at 287.5 eV, 285.45 eV and 284.21 eV, respectively. In the O1s spectrum, four peaks at 531.96 eV, 533.75 eV, 532.79 eV and 531.40 eV corresponded to the PO, P-O, C-O-C and CO groups, respectively [18]. The N1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 399.80 eV and 406.80 eV, which were attributed to pyrrolic N and graphitic N [35], respectively. The P2p spectrum exhibited two peaks at 133.10 eV and 134.80 eV, which were attributed to P-O and PO groups, respectively. The XPS results demonstrated that CDg was a carbon dot doped by P and N. Additionally, the C1s, O1s and N1s high-resolution spectra of CDm were shown in Fig. 1h, where the presence of pyrrolic N in the N1s spectrum meant that CDm was a N-doped carbon dot [35]. Similarly, the XPS spectra showed the differentiation between CDG and CDM (Fig. 1i). Compared to CDG, CDM exhibited lower atomic abundance of N and P and higher abundance of impurities such as Na.

A variety of MXene-based fabrics were fabricated utilizing a straightforward one-pot impregnation method, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. The surface morphology and chemical composition of the fabrics were characterized using SEM, EDS, FTIR and XRD. As observed from the SEM images of Fig. 2b2–b4, the fiber surfaces of all treated fabrics were uniformly coated with MXene. Notably, spherical nano-loaded substances that represented carbon dot mixtures were observed on the surface of both MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L. Fig. S2 showed the EDS mapping of all fabrics. Obviously, the surfaces of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited Ti belonging to MXene and P, N and metal elements belonging to CD mixtures, in addition to C and O elements of C/L. This result indicated that a blended coating of MXene and carbon dot mixtures was successfully constructed on the fabric surface. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 2c and Fig. S3a showed that the –OH peaks (about 3500 cm−1) of all MXene-based fabrics were attenuated and blue-shifted compared to the untreated fabrics, implying that hydrogen bonding was formed between MXene and the fabrics [36,37]. Furthermore, the Ti-O-C stretching vibrational peaks appeared in the spectra of MXene@CDG-C/L (840 cm−1), MXene@CDM-C/L (848 cm−1), MXene@CDg-C/L (842 cm−1) and MXene@CDm-C/L (848 cm−1), which was attributed to the covalent bonding formed by the reaction of MXene with the carbon dots or carbon dots mixture [38]. The XRD further verified the binding between MXene and the carbon dot mixtures or carbon dots (Fig. 2d-e and Fig. S3b). Compared to MXene-C/L, the (002) peaks belonging to MXene in the XRD diffraction patterns of MXene@CDG-C/L, MXene@CDM-C/L, MXene@CDg-C/L and MXene@CDm-C/L were shifted from 6.07° to 5.86°, 5.67°, 5.91°, and 6.03°, respectively, demonstrating that the carbon dots occupied the surface functional groups of Ti3C2Tx MXene and thus Ti3C2Tx MXene hybridized with some of the carbon dots or carbon dot mixtures [30,39]. The combined SEM, FTIR and XRD results indicated that the coatings consisting of MXene with carbon dots or carbon dot mixtures were successfully constructed on the fabric surface.

The vertical burning tests and the LOI tests were used to evaluate the flame retardancy of the fabric samples, and the obtained digital photographs and some key data were shown in Fig. 3 , Fig. S4, Table 1 and Table S1. As shown in Fig. 3a and Table 1, the untreated fabric exhibited a violent and sustained burn upon contact with the flame, ultimately being completely consumed. The after-flame time and after-glow time were observed to be as long as 23 s and 24 s, respectively. Similarly, when the weight gain of CDg-C/L, CDm-C/L, CDM-C/L, CDG-C/L, and MXene-C/L was maintained at about 15 %, their after-glow times were shortened, but still resulted in burnout. Although increasing the amount of these coatings (Table S1 and Figure S4) effectively improved the flame-retardant performance of the fabrics, the significant weight gain inevitably impacted the practical utility of the fabrics. In contrast, MXene@CDm-C/L, MXene@CDg-C/L, MXene@CDM-C/L, and MXene@CDG-C/L demonstrated excellent flame retardancy as their after-flame time and after-glow time were reduced to less than 10 s, while still maintaining a weight gain of approximately 15 %. Consequently, the hybrid coating of Ti3C2Tx MXene with carbon dots or carbon dot mixtures effectively inhibited fabric combustion and mitigated the fire hazard of fabrics.

Interestingly, compared to MXene@CDm-C/L with after-flame time and after-glow time of 10 s and 0 s respectively, MXene@CDg-C/L exhibited superior flame retardancy with after-flame time and after-glow time of 0 s and 1 s. This result indicated that the P, N-doped CDg with ordered crystal structure was more favorable to inhibit the burning of fabrics [20,40]. Furthermore, the char lengths of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L were shortened to 6.7 cm and 5.7 cm, respectively, compared to MXene@CDm-C/L and MXene@CDg-C/L. The result indicated that carbon dot mixtures had a greater flame retardancy advantage over carbon dots alone. The EDS images of the fabrics (Fig. S2) revealed that impurity elements such as Ca and Na belonging to the carbon dot mixtures were additionally added to the surfaces of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L compared to MXene@CDm-C/L and MXene@CDg-C/L. Therefore, the trace impurities in the carbon dot mixtures had a positive impact on the flame retardancy of the fabrics. In addition, the LOI value is an important parameter for evaluating flame retardancy (Fig. 3b). Compared to the LOI value of 18.1 % for C/L, the LOI values of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L increased to 34.4 % and 36.0 %, respectively. Importantly, even after 20 washes, the char length of MXene@CDM-C/Lw and MXene@CDG-C/Lw only was increased by 0.3 cm and 0.5 cm, while the LOI value remained greater than 27 %. Generally, materials with an LOI value greater than 27 % are considered flame retardant. Therefore, the hybrid coatings of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures were stably constructed on the fabric surface, imparting excellent flame retardancy and durability to the fabric.

Based on the comprehensive results of vertical burning and LOI tests, the utilization of carbon dot mixtures instead of pure carbon dots hybridized with MXene for the preparation of flame-retardant coatings offered significant advantages. Firstly, by directly employing the carbon dot mixtures, cumbersome purification steps were avoided, resulting in reduced energy loss. Secondly, the hybridized coatings of carbon dot mixtures with Ti3C2Tx MXene provided the best flame-retardant effect for fabrics with lower weight gain.

Cone calorimetry tests were used to further evaluate the burning performance of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L. The key curves and data were recorded in Fig. 4 and Table 2 . The PHRR and the total heat release (THR) of the untreated fabrics were 172.69 kW m−2 and 11.99 MJ m−2, respectively. In comparison, the PHRR and THR of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L showed different degrees of decrease. A lower PHRR and THR meant a lower fire spread. Therefore, the hybridized coatings of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures effectively enhanced the flame retardancy of the fabrics, thereby reducing the fire hazard of fabrics in large-scale fires. Notably, similar to the vertical burning and LOI test results, MXene@CDG-C/L exhibited lower THR and PHRR than MXene@CDM-C/L. The PHRR and THR of MXene@CDG-C/L were 44.8 kW m−2 and 8.64 MJ m−2, which were 74.1 % and 27.9 % lower than that of C/L, respectively.

In real fires, the release of smoke is a significant hazard. As depicted in Fig. 4 and Table 2, MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L exhibited a notable decrease in total smoke production (TSP). In comparison to the TSP of 0.21 m2 for C/L, the TSP for MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L were 0.05 m2 and 0.16 m2, respectively. It is evident that the hybrid coatings of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures effectively suppressed smoke release. The hybrid coating system consisting of carbon dot mixtures and Ti3C2Tx MXene had a significant catalytic effect on the fabrics, which caused the fabrics to dehydrate at high temperatures to form a char layer, thus inhibiting the heat release [41]. Necessarily, the char residues of MXene-C/L, MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L were significantly increased. Interestingly, MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited lower TSP and SPR compared to MXene@CDG-C/L. This difference can be attributed to the higher concentration of metallic elements present in CDM [42], which effectively captured a larger quantity of gaseous substances at higher temperatures [43].

The thermal degradation behaviors of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L under a N2 atmosphere were investigated using TG tests. The TG curves of the fabrics were shown in Fig. 5 , and the key data for all the fabrics during the degradation process were recorded in Table 3 . The T10% represented the temperature at which a weight loss of 10 % occurred, the Tmax denoted the temperature at which the maximum weight loss occurred, and Rmax indicated the maximum rate of weight loss. MXene-C/L, MXene@CDG-C/L, and MXene@CDM-C/L all demonstrated thermal degradation trends similar to untreated fabrics. However, compared to the T10% of untreated fabrics (149.3 °C), the T10% of all MXene-based fabrics occurred earlier, with T10% of 254.8 °C, 188.1 °C and 203.5 °C for MXene, MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L, respectively. This reason was obtained from the TG curves of MXene, CDM, and CDG. As depicted in Fig. 5c, Fig. S5 and Table S2, the desorption of physically adsorbed water from the surface groups in Ti3C2Tx MXene commenced at 82.0 °C, whereas CDM and CDG exhibited the initial stage of thermal degradation at 76.8 °C and 85.1 °C respectively. Hence, the early occurrence of T10% in all MXene-based fabrics can be attributed to the premature thermal degradation of the Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixture in the coated fabric system. Furthermore, the Tmax of MXene-C/L, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were advanced to 282.7 °C, 206.7 °C and 212.1 °C, respectively, and the corresponding Rmax decreased from 2.23 %/°C to 0.78 %/°C, 0.32 %/°C and 0.33 %/°C, respectively. The observed phenomenon can be attributed to the catalytic effect of the substances produced from the pyrolysis of the Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures, which facilitated the dehydration and carbonization of the fibers, ultimately leading to the formation of a char layer. The char layer acted as a physical barrier to inhibit the exchange of heat thereby reducing the rate of thermal decomposition of the fibers. At 800 °C, the char residues of MXene-C/L, MXene@CDG-C/L, and MXene@CDM-C/L increased to 34.70 wt%, 51.87 wt%, and 46.46 wt%, respectively, compared with that of C/L (15.70 wt%). Consequently, the hybrid coatings of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures effectively altered the thermal stability of the fabric.

To deeply investigate the flame retardation mechanism of the coatings in the condensed phase, the surface elemental composition and structural features of the char residues were comprehensively analyzed using XPS and XRD. As depicted in Fig. 6 and Fig. S6, the char residues of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L not only contained Ti but also exhibited the presence of elements such as P. As a result, both the Ti in Ti3C2Tx MXene and the elements in the CDG and CDM were instrumental in the condensed phase flame retardancy process. The high-resolution Ti2p spectra of MXene@CDG-C/L before and after burning were shown in Fig. 6a and Fig. S7, respectively. Prior to burning, Ti-C and Ti-O bonds were observed at energy levels of 455.0 eV, 458.2 eV, 461.4 eV, and 466.2 eV for Ti2p [29,44]. However, following burning, only the Ti2p1/2 (466.3 eV) and Ti2p3/2 (460.2 eV) bound states associated with Ti-O groups were evident in the Ti2p spectrum. The XRD patterns presented in Fig. 6b illustrated that the char residues of MXene@CDG-C/L exhibited peaks at 2θ = 27.4°, 36.1°, and 41.7°, which correspond to rutile TiO2, along with a peak at 2θ = 25.3°, indicating the presence of anatase TiO2 [42]. According to the report, TiO2 acted both as a physical barrier to isolate heat and oxygen during polymer pyrolysis and as a solid acid to perform good catalytic carbonization [45]. In addition, the high-resolution P2p spectrum of the corresponding MXene@CDG-C/L char residue showed phosphorus-containing components such as PO and P-O-C [4]. Although these components were in trace amounts, they still further contributed to the dehydration carbonization of the fabric. Similarly, MXene@CDM-C/L has the same flame-retardant mechanism.

TG-IR was used to study gaseous volatiles produced by pyrolysis of fabrics. It was evident from the 3D images in Fig. 6c and Fig. S8 that the gaseous volatiles released from the three MXene-based fabrics all exhibited a significant change in absorbance intensity, as distinguished from the untreated C/L. Fig. 6d-g displayed some gaseous volatiles including CO (1734 cm−1), C-H (2975 cm−1), C-O-C (1063 cm−1) and CO2 (2361 cm−1) at specific wavenumbers as a function of time [8]. Obviously, compared to the untreated C/L, all MXene-based fabrics released flammable volatiles such as CO, C-H and C-O-C in advance, which was attributed to the fact that Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dots had relatively low pyrolysis temperatures. Fortunately, pyrolysis products from Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dots catalysed the dehydration and carbonization of the matrix, thus preventing the cellulose from releasing more flammable volatiles at higher temperatures. Because of this, all MXene-based fabrics showed reduced absorbance intensities for CO, C-H and C-O-C. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 6g, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L released additional CO2 at a later stage of thermal decomposition compared to C/L and MXene-C/L, indicating that the carbon dots underwent further pyrolysis at higher temperatures. Moreover, CO2 was considered a non-flammable volatile and its increased release contributed to the dilution of the concentration of flammable volatiles.

Combining all the test results, a possible flame-retardant mechanism was proposed and presented in Fig. 6h. Both carbon dot mixtures and Ti3C2Tx MXene contributed to the condensed phase and gas phase flame retardation process. In the condensed phase, on the one hand, Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dots served as a thermal and oxygen barrier at lower temperatures. On the other hand, Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures further generated some metal oxides and phosphorus-containing as the temperature increased. These substances further catalyzed the dehydration and carbonization of the fabrics, resulting in the formation of a dense carbon layer. In the gas phase, the carbon dots gradually oxidized into non-flammable volatiles (such as CO2). CO2 effectively diluted the concentration of flammable volatiles, thereby reducing the fire risk. In conclusion, Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures exhibited a remarkable synergistic effect on flame retardancy.

Fig. 7 a demostrated the small lamp luminescence in the closed pathway of the MXene@CDG-C/L connection. Obviously, the small lamp started to light up when the fabric was connected to the positive and negative ends, which is a full indication that MXene@CDG-C/L has conductive properties. Conductivity tests were performed on all fabrics and the results were recorded in Fig. 7b and Fig. S9. In comparison to the conductivities of MXene-C/L(16.4 S m−1), CDg-C/L (0.22 S m−1), CDg-C/L (0.1 S m−1), CDG-C/L (0.9 S m−1), CDM-C/L (1.1 S m−1), MXene@CDg-C/L, MXene@CDm-C/L, MXene@CDG-C/L, and MXene@CDM-C/L exhibited electrical conductivities of 30.0 S m−1, 20.8 S m−1, 31.8 S m−1, and 21.4 S m−1, respectively. It is evident that there was also a certain synergistic promotion between Ti3C2Tx MXene and the carbon dots or carbon dots mixtures in terms of electrical properties. The possible conductive mechanisms of MXene-based fabrics were shown in Fig. 7c. MXene formed a continuous coating on the fabric surface, which created closed pathways for the electrons [46]. Simultaneously, the carbon dots mixtures increased the electron channels between neighbouring MXene lamellae, and thus the electrical conductivity of the fabric was significantly increased [29]. Interestingly, the conductivity of MXene@CDG-C/Lw and MXene@CDM-C/Lw remained higher than that of MXene-C/L after washing due to the strong interactions between Ti3C2Tx MXene, carbon dot mixtures, and C/L.

Fig. 7a showed that the brightness of the small lamp changed significantly when the MXene-based fabric was deformed, implying the presence of sensing properties in the MXene-based fabrics. Consequently, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were adhered to human fingers and palm joints to detect the sensing ability of the fabrics. Fig. 7d-g recorded the rate of change of the resistance of the fabric during the bending of the finger and the clenching of the fist (ΔR/R 0, R 0 was the resistance of the fabric at 0°, R was the resistance of the fabric during motion, and ΔR was the difference between the resistance of the fabric during motion and R 0.) [47]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the resistance change rate of MXene@CDG-C/L was maintained at about 110 % when the finger was in the 0° state and decreased to about 65 % when the finger was rapidly bent to 90°. Interestingly, when the finger was restored from 90° to 0°, the resistance change rate was again about 110 %. The test results indicated that the MXene@CDG-C/L was able to effectively detect finger movement and was able to repeatedly acquire motion-sensing signals. Similarly, the resistance change rate of MXene@CDG-C/L was maintained between 100 % and 53 % when a fist-clenching manoeuvre was performed (Fig. 7e). In addition, as shown in Fig. 7f-g and Fig. S10, MXene@CDM-C/L, MXene@CDM-C/Lw, and MXene@CDG-C/Lw exhibited superior performance in detecting human motion. The sensing mechanism of MXene-based fabrics was given in Fig. 7h. When a flexible fabric was subjected to an external force, the fiber-to-fiber distance, the MXene-to-MXene distance, the MXene-to-CDs distance, and the MXene-to-fiber distance in the fabric were changed. This results in a complex change in the electron transport channels, and therefore in a change in the electrical resistance. And when the external force was withdrawn, the electronic channel quickly returned to its original state [46]. In summary, the flexibility of C/L and the conductivity of hybrid coating system brought desirable motion-sensing properties to MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L.

The functional fabrics in this work were compared with the multifunctional fabrics that have been published in recent years in Fig. 8 a [46,48–53]. In contrast, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were prepared by a simple one-pot impregnation method that avoided the layer-by-layer self-assembly process used in most of the work, which resulted in lower flame-retardant coating loadings for MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L. Although the LOI values of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were not outstanding compared to the other work, they had higher conductivity and sensing (The sensing signals are briefly evaluated by the difference between the maximum and minimum values of ΔR/R 0) ability while achieving inter-functional promotion and coordination. At the same time, MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L still had the desired multi-functionality after 20 washes.

Fabrics should not only have good washing durability in reality but also ensure certain mechanical properties. Fig. 8b recorded the conductivity of MXene@CDG-C/L after repeated folding. The conductivity of MXene@CDG-C/L changed only slightly after 10 foldings, thus showing that MXene@CDG-C/L retained the softness of the original fabric. This softness further guaranteed the actual use of the fabric. The tensile properties of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L in the warp and weft directions were demonstrated in Fig. 8c-d. Compared to C/L, the tensile strengths of MXene@CDG-C/L and MXene@CDM-C/L were significantly enhanced in the warp and weft directions by 31.2 %, 22.8 % and 19.2 %, 11.5 %, respectively. A possible mechanism for mechanical property enhancement was illustrated in Fig. 8e. The improvement in tensile properties was attributed to the formation of strong hydrogen bonding interactions between the Ti3C2Tx MXene, the carbon dots mixture and the cellulose fabric. Moreover, the increase in tensile strength was also favoured by the fact that the 2D MXene transferred a portion of the stress to the 0D carbon dots, which then continued to dissipate energy in a directional manner [54].

In this paper, two multifunctional cellulosic fabrics named MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L were prepared using homemade carbon dot mixtures derived from grapefruit peel or mulberry and Ti3C2Tx MXene by the one-pot impregnation method. On the one hand, MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L exhibited good flame retardancy due to the synergistic effect of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures in the condensed and gas phases. The vertical combustion test showed that the damaged lengths of MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L were as low as 6.7 cm and 5.7 cm, respectively. The cone calorimetry test demonstrated that MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L released less heat and smoke compared to the control C/L. The TG analysis confirmed that the intervention of Ti3C2Tx MXene and carbon dot mixtures promoted richer char residue formed by cellulose. On the other hand, MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L had fine electrical conductivity and sensing properties. MXene@CDM-C/L and MXene@CDG-C/L exhibited conductivity of 21.4 S m−1 and 31.8 S m−1, respectively, and were able to detect human movement in a sensitive and continuous manner. Critically, the integrated properties of the two multifunctional cellulose fabrics, including flame retardancy, electrical conductivity and sensing properties, remained higher than those of the control C/L after washing. In conclusion, the simple and environment-friendly 0D@2D coatings for cellulosic fabrics modification reported in this work provide an alternative route for the development of new multifunctional textiles.
